editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to tighten up my writing. What’s a quick way to spot sentences that are too long or unclear?

A practical test is to read each sentence out loud in one breath—if you can’t, it’s likely too long. Also look for multiple ideas joined by “and/while/which/that”; split into two sentences and make the subject and verb show up early.
Got it. Any simple structure you recommend for short answers to application questions?
Try a 3-part structure: (1) one-line headline point, (2) one concrete example or proof, (3) link back to what the role needs. Keeping it to ~80–120 words forces clarity.
Can you give me an example of turning a vague sentence into something more specific?

Vague: “I improved the project significantly.” Specific: “I reduced the test cycle from 3 days to 1 day by standardising the checklist and automating data capture, which helped the team run two extra iterations before the deadline.”
That helps. Random question: what’s a fun bit of engineering history you like?

The 1940 Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse is a classic: it wasn’t “just wind,” but an aeroelastic flutter instability. It changed how engineers think about vibration, damping, and wind-structure interaction, and it still shows up in textbooks today.
Can you tweak that hobbies paragraph so it sounds more natural for me—like the bit about football is more “weekend matches with mates” than anything serious?

Sure—here’s a more natural, down-to-earth version that keeps it credible:

Outside of my Mechanical Engineering degree, I keep active through the gym and football. I’m consistent with training and enjoy the discipline of powerlifting, and I also play football regularly—more weekend matches with friends than anything competitive—but I like the teamwork and routine it brings.

I’m also genuinely interested in cars and aircraft, and I often end up reading or watching content about how performance and efficiency are achieved through design choices. Those interests keep me curious outside of lectures and link closely to why I enjoy engineering in the first place.
quick correction before you write it up: i actually don’t play football anymore — i used to, but now it’s mostly just the gym/powerlifting.
Got it — I’ll focus it on gym/powerlifting as your main sport, and only mention football in the past tense (something you used to do), not as a current hobby.
